Maybe it would be a good idea to add a specialized implementation when it is called on a `SkipMissing`, as there is for `sum`. The easiest way to do this would be to pass a function that computes both `sum` and `count` to `mapreduce`.

```julia
A = rand(Float32, 1_000_000) .+ 1f6
mean(skipmissing(A)) ≈ mean(A) # false
mean(A) ≈ (sum(skipmissing(A)) / length(A)) # true
```

s]"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":10,"created_at":"2020-06-16T17:53:21Z","updated_at":"2024-11-06T16:01:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Preface: I know, whining about 64 bytes and 30ns is petty, but Julia makes me greedy!
 
```julia
@btime reshape($(rand(3,3)), :)
@btime reshape($(rand(3,3)), (9,))

#+RESULTS:
:   37.752 ns (2 allocations: 80 bytes)
:   27.638 ns (1 allocation: 64 bytes)
: 9-element Array{Float64,1}:
:  0.4804836997299047
:  0.936716866167745
:  0.9977577878061075
:  0.8811141421915005
:  0.17675166366526107
:  0.5546267273015555
:  0.6343021733102954
:  0.13541413200323893
:  0.7387791437220763
```
```julia
versioninfo()

#+RESULTS:
Julia Version 1.5.0-beta1.0
Commit 6443f6c95a* (2020-05-28 17:42 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu)
  CPU: AMD Ryzen 5 2600 Six-Core Processor
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-9.0.1 (ORCJIT, znver1)
Environment:
  JULIA_NUM_THREADS = 6
````
Unless I'm missing something, I think this should be possible with much lower overhead because nothing needs to change in the data, just the type and dropping of the dims. For instance, UnsafeArrays.jl can do it (unsafely) an order of magnitude quicker and without allocating, but I don't think it needs to be unsafe to be so quick. 
```julia
using UnsafeArrays
@btime uview($(rand(3,3)), :)
@btime uview($(rand(3,3)), 1:9)

#+RESULTS:
:   1.550 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
:   2.579 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
: 9-element UnsafeArray{Float64,1}:
:  0.46310146763916626
:  0.05182264678216142
:  0.14274914955937046
:  0.2758767433411191
:  0.2363350986596353
:  0.17657284176321464
:  0.4472773750902235
:  0.6573104601143722
:  0.3421327197227122
```

I would argue that this is a true statement no matter what we document. Branch-cutting on `+0.0` vs `-0.0` is probably the only legitimate "generic" use for `signbit` and even then it's only applicable for the subset of types that support signed zeros. Otherwise it's for bit-twiddling and inspecting `NaN`s (neither of which are "generic use").

If you want to check `<(0)`, you should *not* use `signbit` because it's not the same function and it doesn't have the same semantics.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2483545669/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

r, a, y, s]"},{"id":5819899318,"node_id":"LA_kwDOABkWpM8AAAABWuSdtg","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/correctness%20bug%20%E2%9A%A0","name":"correctness bug ⚠","color":"000000","default":false,"description":"Bugs that are likely to lead to incorrect results in user code without throwing"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":27,"created_at":"2021-01-24T20:54:34Z","updated_at":"2024-11-20T15:55:32Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I noticed that `sum!` and `prod!` do not check for aliasing, leading to incorrect results when the same array is passed in both arguments. The incorrect behavior under aliasing is not documented and the result is a silent unexpected wrong result.

```julia
julia> a = [1 2 3];

julia> sum!(copy(a), a)
1×3 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2  3

julia> sum!(a, a)
1×3 Matrix{Int64}:
 0  0  0

julia> a = [1 2 3];

julia> prod!(copy(a), a)
1×3 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2  3

julia> prod!(a, a)
1×3 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  1  1
```

Not all mutating functions have this problem and `cumsum!` can be used  this way without error:

```julia
julia> a = [1, 2, 3];

julia> cumsum!(a, a)
3-element Vector{Int64}:
 1
 3
 6
```

Other functions correctly flag the aliasing in the case of `===` arguments and throw an error:

```julia
julia> using LinearAlgebra

julia> a = rand(2, 2);

julia> mul!(a, a, a)
ERROR: ArgumentError: output matrix must not be aliased with input matrix
```

The examples above were generated on Julia `1.6.0-beta1` and I see the same behavior in version `1.5.3`.

Edit: I noticed this issue also applies to `any!` and `all!`:

```julia
julia> let a = [true, false]
           any!(a, a)
       end
2-element Array{Bool,1}:
 0
 0

julia> let a = [true, false]
           any!(copy(a), a)
       end
2-element Array{Bool,1}:
 1
 0

julia> let a = [true, false]
           all!(a, a)
       end
2-element Array{Bool,1}:
 1
 1

julia> let a = [true, false]
           all!(copy(a), a)
       end
2-element Array{Bool,1}:
 1
 0

throwing"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":29,"created_at":"2021-01-24T20:54:34Z","updated_at":"2024-11-22T16:05:00Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I noticed that `sum!` and `prod!` do not check for aliasing, leading to incorrect results when the same array is passed in both arguments. The incorrect behavior under aliasing is not documented and the result is a silent unexpected wrong result.

```julia
julia> a = [1 2 3];

julia> sum!(copy(a), a)
1×3 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2  3

julia> sum!(a, a)
1×3 Matrix{Int64}:
 0  0  0

julia> a = [1 2 3];

julia> prod!(copy(a), a)
1×3 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2  3

julia> prod!(a, a)
1×3 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  1  1
```

Not all mutating functions have this problem and `cumsum!` can be used  this way without error:

```julia
julia> a = [1, 2, 3];

julia> cumsum!(a, a)
3-element Vector{Int64}:
 1
 3
 6
```

Other functions correctly flag the aliasing in the case of `===` arguments and throw an error:

```julia
julia> using LinearAlgebra

julia> a = rand(2, 2);

julia> mul!(a, a, a)
ERROR: ArgumentError: output matrix must not be aliased with input matrix
```

The examples above were generated on Julia `1.6.0-beta1` and I see the same behavior in version `1.5.3`.

Edit: I noticed this issue also applies to `any!` and `all!`:

```julia
julia> let a = [true, false]
           any!(a, a)
       end
2-element Array{Bool,1}:
 0
 0

julia> let a = [true, false]
           any!(copy(a), a)
       end
2-element Array{Bool,1}:
 1
 0

julia> let a = [true, false]
           all!(a, a)
       end
2-element Array{Bool,1}:
 1
 1

julia> let a = [true, false]
           all!(copy(a), a)
       end
2-element Array{Bool,1}:
 1
 0
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39385/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39385/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2494106152","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39385#issuecomment-2494106152","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39385","id":2494106152,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6UqQoo","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-22T16:04:59Z","updated_at":"2024-11-22T16:04:59Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> if closed it should be closed as "not planned" rather than as "completed"

The issue is about *silently* returning wrong results. Now that there's an explicit warning in the docstring (for these functions and many more that weren't even part of this issue), I wouldn't classify it as "silently incorrect" any more than `@fastmath` (whose warnings are much more muted and whose failure modes are not clearly explained) or any `unsafe_XYZ` function.

Closing this as "not planned" doesn't seem right, especially since this is tagged "correctness bug." I don't think it's any issue that a function misbehaves when explicitly documented argument requirements are violated. If we want an issue for accurate and fast alias detection (which would be necessary to "100% close" this issue), that sounds like a separate feature request (and maybe a pipe dream).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2494106152/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

speculative"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2022-07-25T13:28:22Z","updated_at":"2024-11-26T11:42:44Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"From this [Discourse discussion](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/possible-bug-with-operator/84753).

```julia
using LinearAlgebra

julia> ones(3,2) \ ones(3)
2-element Vector{Float64}:
 0.5
 0.4999999999999999

julia> ones(3,3) \ ones(3)
ERROR: SingularException(2)

julia> ones(3,4) \ ones(3)
4-element Vector{Float64}:
 0.2500000000000001
 0.25
 0.25
 0.25
```

The issue here is that `\` applies the LU decomposition to a square input but a pivoted QR to a rectangular input. The LU solver cannot handle singular inputs while QR can.

```julia
julia> qr(ones(3,3),ColumnNorm()) \ ones(3)
3-element Vector{Float64}:
 0.33333333333333337
 0.33333333333333337
 0.33333333333333337
```

A naive fix might just be to check the `lu(A)` call within `\` and re-try with `qr(A,ColumnNorm())` if it fails via singularity.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/939/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/939/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1024","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1024/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1024/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1024/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1024","id":2694377422,"node_id":"I_kwDONPNfxc6gmO_O","number":1024,"title":"`LinearAlgebra.QRCompactWYQ` from `qr` has incomplete support for matrix division","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2023-09-20T17:04:50Z","updated_at":"2024-11-26T11:45:23Z","closed_at":"2024-05-21T15:07:02Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Julia `v1.9.1`.

The Q factor from `qr` has some flexibility in the size of matrix it is multiplied with. Nominally if `Q,R = qr(X)` with `size(X) == (m,n)` then it is permitted to multiply `Q` with a matrix/vector with dimension  `m` *or* `n`.

However, this support breaks down very quickly. Errors currently result from attempts ~~to multiply `Q` by an `Adjoint` or~~ (EDIT: fixed in v1.10) to "divide" it by another matrix when the other matrix has adjoining dimension `!=(m)` (i.e., the "full" dimension of `Q`), even though dimension `n` should be accepted.
```julia
julia> using LinearAlgebra

julia> X = Float64[1;2;3;; 4; 5; 6;]
3×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.0  4.0
 2.0  5.0
 3.0  6.0

julia> Q,R = qr(X)
LinearAlgebra.QRCompactWY{Float64, Matrix{Float64}, Matrix{Float64}}
Q factor:
3×3 LinearAlgebra.QRCompactWYQ{Float64, Matrix{Float64}, Matrix{Float64}}:
 -0.267261   0.872872   0.408248
 -0.534522   0.218218  -0.816497
 -0.801784  -0.436436   0.408248
R factor:
2×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 -3.74166  -8.55236
  0.0       1.96396

julia> Q*X
3×2 Matrix{Float64}:
  2.70323   5.7448
 -2.54758  -5.94598
 -0.44991  -2.93982

julia> Q*collect(X')
3×3 Matrix{Float64}:
  3.22423    3.82984     4.43545
  0.338349   0.0220445  -0.29426
 -2.54753   -3.78575    -5.02397

julia> Q*X' # worked when Adjoint was collected, but not raw -- EDIT: fixed v1.10
ERROR: DimensionMismatch: first dimensions of C, 2, and V, 3 must match
Stacktrace:
 [1] gemqrt!(side::Char, trans::Char, V::Matrix{Float64}, T::Matrix{Float64}, C::Matrix{Float64})
   @ LinearAlgebra.LAPACK ~/julia-1.9.1/share/julia/stdlib/v1.9/LinearAlgebra/src/lapack.jl:2916
 [2] lmul!
   @ ~/julia-1.9.1/share/julia/stdlib/v1.9/LinearAlgebra/src/qr.jl:626 [inlined]
 [3] *(Q::LinearAlgebra.QRCompactWYQ{Float64, Matrix{Float64}, Matrix{Float64}}, adjB::Adjoint{Float64, Matrix{Float64}})
   @ LinearAlgebra ~/julia-1.9.1/share/julia/stdlib/v1.9/LinearAlgebra/src/qr.jl:735
 [4] top-level scope
   @ REPL[157]:1

julia> Q/R # but Q*R works (obviously), as does Q*inv(R)
ERROR: DimensionMismatch: Both inputs should have the same number of columns
Stacktrace:
 [1] /(A::LinearAlgebra.QRCompactWYQ{Float64, Matrix{Float64}, Matrix{Float64}}, B::Matrix{Float64})
   @ LinearAlgebra ~/julia-1.9.1/share/julia/stdlib/v1.9/LinearAlgebra/src/generic.jl:1146
 [2] top-level scope
   @ REPL[158]:1
```
A current workaround is to slice `Q` to the appropriate dimension (manually) prior to the unsupported operation. It seems that this fix could be applied generally, although I have not yet explored how many methods may require fixing.

This came up when I was attempting to use `R\Q'` to compute `pinv(X)` for `m>n==rank(X)`, since `pinv` uses `svd` which has poor support for general element types (such as `ForwardDiff.Dual`).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1024/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1024/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1043","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1043/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1043/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1043/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1043","id":2694378800,"node_id":"I_kwDONPNfxc6gmPUw","number":1043,"title":"Re-associating matrix-chain multiplication yields unpredictable results","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2023-12-04T17:50:52Z","updated_at":"2024-11-26T11:45:59Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Version 1.9.4.

```
*(A, B::AbstractMatrix, C)
  A * B * C * D

  Chained multiplication of 3 or 4 matrices is done in the most efficient sequence, based on the sizes of the
  arrays. That is, the number of scalar multiplications needed for (A * B) * C (with 3 dense matrices) is
  compared to that for A * (B * C) to choose which of these to execute.

  If the last factor is a vector, or the first a transposed vector, then it is efficient to deal with these
  first. In particular x' * B * y means (x' * B) * y for an ordinary column-major B::Matrix. Unlike dot(x, B,
  y), this allocates an intermediate array.

  If the first or last factor is a number, this will be fused with the matrix multiplication, using 5-arg mul!.

  See also muladd, dot.

  │ Julia 1.7
  │
  │  These optimisations require at least Julia 1.7.
```

Obviously, this transformation slightly changes results for floating point numbers due to their mild non-associativity. However, it is also applied in contexts with mathematics that are decisively non-associative. For example:
```julia-repl
julia> using Octonions

julia> x = Octonion(1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8); y = Octonion(1,-2,3,4,5,6,7,8); z = Octonion(1,2,-3,4,5,6,7,8);

julia> [x;;] * [y;;] * [z;;] # left-associative evaluation
1×1 Matrix{Octonion{Int64}}:
 Octonion{Int64}(-652, -1064, 612, -736, -860, -1128, -1036, -1712)

julia> [x;;] * [y;;] * [z;;] * [1;] # trailing vector causes right-associativity
1-element Vector{Octonion{Int64}}:
 Octonion{Int64}(-652, -1064, 612, -736, -1148, -888, -1420, -1376)
```

We like to compose packages in Julia. Arbitrary re-assocation is incompatible with this. Since "standard" associativity is undefined, it becomes necessary for a "correct" package to explicitly force every association with parentheses. N-ary evaluation cannot be correctly applied without tight control of the context that ensures re-association is mathematically valid. The presence of re-association in any insufficiently-controlled context (like the above matrix-chain specialization) will lead to correctness issues across the ecosystem.

Spiritually related to JuliaLang/julia#52333.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1043/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1043/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

`BunchKaufman`","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-06-06T14:29:41Z","updated_at":"2024-11-26T11:47:00Z","closed_at":"2024-06-13T13:33:17Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Julia v1.10.3

```julia-repl
julia> x = rand(3,3);

julia> factorize(x'*x) |> typeof
Cholesky{Float64, Matrix{Float64}}

julia> factorize(Hermitian(x'*x)) |> typeof
BunchKaufman{Float64, Matrix{Float64}, Vector{Int64}}
```

The first thing `factorize(x'*x)` does is determine that `x'*x` is structurally hermitian. Once it does, it attempts `cholesky`. If that fails, it calls `factorize(Hermitian(x'*x))`.

But when `factorize(Hermitian(x'*x))` is called (either directly or after determining that `x'*x` is structurally hermitian), `cholesky` is never attempted. Instead, it attempts `Diagonal`, then `bunchkaufman` or `lu` depending on the `eltype`.

This is at odds with `@doc factorize`, which says `cholesky` is favored for positive definite matrices (which are necessarily `ishermitian`, if not `Hermitian`).

Ignoring the documentation discrepancy, it seems that there shouldn't be this distinction. Either `cholesky` should be preferred for all `ishermitian` matrices or we should never try at all. The fact that the `factorize(<:Hermitian)` method does *not* attempt `cholesky` makes me think someone thought about this and preferred "never cholesky".

However, `bunchkaufman` is 1.5-2x slower than `cholesky` (default OpenBLAS, sizes $3 \times 3$ to $500 \times 500$) and appears to even be somewhat slower than `lu` (1.0-1.5x). It seems that `bunchkaufman` is either overrated or has some low-hanging performance improvements available.

`cholesky` has the potential to be faster, but work is wasted if it fails. `lu` is still faster than `bunchkaufman` (but algorithmically this seems backwards, so *could* reverse in the future), but forgets the hermitian structure. I'm not sure what should be preferred, but I think a consistent preference would be better than the current situation. I guess my dream world would have `bunchkaufman` nearly as fast as `cholesky` and we would just use that (since `cholesky` can still fail for many nonsingular matrices).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1076/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1076/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

julia> VERSION
v"1.10.3"

julia> [1 1e-10; 0 1] ^ 1e20
ERROR: InexactError: Int64(1.0e20)
```
The issue is that there are multiple places where our exponentiation routines attempt to convert (or round, in some places) the exponent to an `Int` without first checking that such a conversion is possible. For example, in `^`, we call `LinearAlgebra.integerpow` on `isinteger` powers (including all large float powers) which attempts `Integer(p)`. In `LinearAlgebra.schurpow` we attempt `floor(Integer, p)` which fails for the same reasons. There may be additional sites with the unchecked assumption that one can convert to `Int`, but I noticed those two.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1082/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1082/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

idea here is that `A^p` is evaluated as `A^Int(p)` if `isinteger(p)`, but `Int(p)` may error if `p` is large. In such cases, we may express the exponent as `p = m*q + r`, where `m` and `r` are small enough so that `Int(m)` and `Int(r)` succeed, and the operation becomes `(A^m)^q * A^r`. This is evaluated recursively by repeatedly dividing the exponent. 

After this, the following works:
```julia
julia> [1 1e-10; 0 1] ^ (1e20)
2×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.0  1.0e10
 0.0  1.0
```

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/1082, but I have not carried out any error analysis to check the accuracy of results in general. Suggestions are welcome. In particular, I'm unsure how the rounding in `divrem` impacts the results if the exponents are huge.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55431/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":3,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55431/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2505074333","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55431#issuecomment-2505074333","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55431","id":2505074333,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6VUGad","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-28T01:06:01Z","updated_at":"2024-11-28T01:06:01Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> just treat `p` the same as any floating-point exponent if it's too big?

faster"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":19,"created_at":"2022-06-04T17:56:48Z","updated_at":"2024-10-09T13:47:00Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/45581","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/45581","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/45581.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/45581.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR uses a new method to greatly accelerate `minimum`/`maximum`/`extrema` for `IEEEFloat` values. The concept is to convert floats to signed integers that respect the same ordering that `min`/`max` do for floats. Since integers can be min/max'd much more quickly than floats, this leads to a considerable speedup. This is a minor extension to the concept used by `isless` since #39090. I used the changes here to slightly accelerate that version of `isless` as well.

Note that the old versions had special-cased `_mapreduce_impl` for `min`/`max`. The reckless application of these led to very poor performance for integer `minimum`/`maximum`. This implementation eliminates those specializations, improving integer performance as well.

Benchmark:
```
using BenchmarkTools
a = randn(1000);
b = zeros(1000);
c = @. ifelse(rand() < 0.1, missing, a);
j = rand(Int,1000);
@btime minimum($a); # "typical" case
@btime minimum($b); # pathological case for old version
@btime extrema($a);
@btime minimum($c); # with missing
@btime minimum($j); # integer performance
@btime searchsortedlast($a, x) setup=(x=randn()); # isless performance
```

Before:
```
  812.222 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  1.620 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  4.629 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  1.220 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  262.315 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  14.729 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```
This PR:
```
  173.764 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  173.721 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  278.276 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  954.839 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  92.453 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  11.311 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```
~#43725:~ EDIT: never mind - it sounds like I didn't get that benchmark right

Results are similar for `maximum`.
This idea does not support values that may be `Missing` whereas the original specialization did. Interestingly, eliminating that specialization (none of the new code here is run with `Missing`) still led to a performance improvement.

For `Float64`, this PR makes `minimum`/`maximum` 4.7x faster (more in pathological case), `extrema` 16x faster, and `isless` 1.3x faster. Note that `sort` already has specializations for floats, which remains faster than the naive application of this updated `isless`. This also makes `minimum`/`maximum` 2.8x faster for `Int`.


The technical idea here is finished, but I could use some help on the architecture. The current version makes `_mapreduce(f,op,...)` check for whether a registered acceleration function exists for `op` for the type of `f` applied to the return type. It only takes effect if the `return_type` of `f` applied to the input is known to be a `IEEEFloat`. In the case that such a function exists, it wraps `f` in the accelerating transformation and applies an appropriate inverse transformation to the result. Even if the architectural idea is sound, I'm not sure I put the corresponding functions in reasonable places within the code.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45581/reactions","total_count":4,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":4,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45581/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2402399671","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/45581#issuecomment-2402399671","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45581","id":2402399671,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6PMbW3","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-09T13:46:59Z","updated_at":"2024-10-09T13:46:59Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I'm increasingly un-enamored with this PR. However, I still think that the current specializations should still be removed (or at least limited only to `IEEEFloat`) and the `isless` improvement seems fine.

But the rest of it is a lot of code and complication (and maintenance) for some modest improvements. Further, I suspect that this would be a regression on AMD systems where the desired `min`/`max` semantics are available as native instructions. 

Currently, the `reduce` family of functions are just too complicated to make specializations like this palatable. I suspect that's why those of us that worked on this eventually burned out. And there's further work happening on those families (universal pairwise `reduce`, changes to `init` semantics) that may some adjustment here.

But feel free to pick this up and get it over the line yourself, if you like. It would be easier (almost done, probably?) if you abandoned dimensional reduction and merely aimed to improve the whole-collection case.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2402399671/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56325","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56325/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56325/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56325/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56325","id":2614030526,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6bzvC-","number":56325,"title":"Improving MethodError message for anonymous functions","user":{"login":"fonsp","id":6933510,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY5MzM1MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6933510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp","html_url":"https://github.com/fonsp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-10-25T12:52:59Z","updated_at":"2024-10-25T14:13:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"*Context: improving error experience for beginner Julia programmers.*

In this example, you get a `MethodError` for your anonymous method (`::var"#3#4"`):

```julia-repl
julia> vals = [5,6,10,20];

julia> accumulate(vals; init=0) do (old, new)
           old + new
       end
```
```
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching (::var"#3#4")(::Int64, ::Int64)
The function `#3` exists, but no method is defined for this combination of argument types.

Closest candidates are:
  (::var"#3#4")(::Any)
   @ Main REPL[2]:2

Stacktrace:
 [1] _accumulate!(op::var"#3#4", B::Vector{Union{}}, A::Vector{Int64}, dims::Nothing, init::Some{Int64})
   @ Base ./accumulate.jl:368
 [2] accumulate!(op::Function, B::Vector{Union{}}, A::Vector{Int64}; dims::Nothing, kw::@Kwargs{init::Int64})
   @ Base ./accumulate.jl:350
 [3] accumulate(op::Function, A::Vector{Int64}; dims::Nothing, kw::@Kwargs{init::Int64})
   @ Base ./accumulate.jl:291
 [4] top-level scope
   @ REPL[2]:1
```

The problem is that my `do` syntax used `(old, new)` (a single tuple argument) instead of `old, new` (two integer arguments):

```julia-repl
julia> accumulate(vals; init=0) do old, new
           old + new
       end
4-element Vector{Int64}:
  5
 11
 21
 41
```

But the error is difficult to understand!

# Ideas
I have seen this pattern more often (`MethodError` on an anonymous function that I defined), and I think we could make meaningful improvements to this error message! Ideas:
1. **Say "anonymous function":** We can communicate better that it's about an "anonymous function". The name `var"#3#4"` is not so useful for beginners. (In the case of a `do`-block, it might even be non-obvious that you wrote a *function*.)
2. **Simplify "Closest candidates":** Anonymous functions can only have one method (right?). So instead of showing "Closest candidates", the error can be stated in a simpler way:
  ```
  The anonymous function has signature:
     (::Any)
  but it was called with types:
     (::Int64, ::Int64)
  ```
3. **Number of arguments:** the function was called with **2** arguments, but no method accepts that. When dispatch fails because of the *number* of arguments, I think this is the most helpful bit of debugging info available (like a sanity check), and we could say this explicitly in the error. 
  It could be phrased as advice (when the situation is simple enough): 
  ```
  Tip: The function was called with 2 arguments, but it only accepts 1 argument.
  ```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56325/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56325/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2437919194","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56325#issuecomment-2437919194","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56325","id":2437919194,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6RT7Ha","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-25T14:13:03Z","updated_at":"2024-10-25T14:13:03Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Just a nitpick, but anonymous functions can have multiple methods. But I will gripe that `in`'s ambiguity on the subject of `==` vs `isequal` is awful:
```julia-repl
  in(item, collection) -> Bool
  ∈(item, collection) -> Bool

  Determine whether an item is in the given collection, in the sense that it is == to one of the values generated by
  iterating over the collection. Return a Bool value, except if item is missing or collection contains missing but not
  item, in which case missing is returned (three-valued logic (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Three-valued_logic),
  matching the behavior of any and ==).

  Some collections follow a slightly different definition. For example, Sets check whether the item isequal to one of
  the elements; Dicts look for key=>value pairs, and the key is compared using isequal.
```

I find the choice of `==` to be questionable with regard to `AbstractFloat` behavior
```julia-repl
julia> -0.0 in [+0.0] # debatable, but if I wanted `==` or `a <= b <= c` I probably should have written that
true

julia> NaN in [NaN] # these are `===`
false
```
and think the fact that `Set` (and others?) has different behavior is abhorrent
```julia-repl
julia> -0.0 in Set([+0.0])
false

julia> NaN in Set([NaN])
true
```

; ┌ @ tuple.jl:31 within `getindex`
   %"a::Tuple[9]_ptr" = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %"a::Tuple", i64 64
   %"b::Tuple[3]_ptr" = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %"b::Tuple", i64 16
   %"a::Tuple[6]_ptr" = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %"a::Tuple", i64 40
   %"b::Tuple[2]_ptr" = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %"b::Tuple", i64 8
   %"a::Tuple[3]_ptr" = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %"a::Tuple", i64 16
; └
; ┌ @ float.jl:493 within `*`
   %"a::Tuple[3]_ptr.unbox" = load double, ptr %"a::Tuple[3]_ptr", align 8
   %"b::Tuple.unbox" = load double, ptr %"b::Tuple", align 8
   %0 = fmul contract double %"a::Tuple[3]_ptr.unbox", %"b::Tuple.unbox"
; └
; ┌ @ float.jl:496 within `muladd`
   %"a::Tuple[6]_ptr.unbox" = load double, ptr %"a::Tuple[6]_ptr", align 8
   %"b::Tuple[2]_ptr.unbox" = load double, ptr %"b::Tuple[2]_ptr", align 8
   %1 = fmul contract double %"a::Tuple[6]_ptr.unbox", %"b::Tuple[2]_ptr.unbox"
   %2 = fadd contract double %0, %1
   %"a::Tuple[9]_ptr.unbox" = load double, ptr %"a::Tuple[9]_ptr", align 8
   %"b::Tuple[3]_ptr.unbox" = load double, ptr %"b::Tuple[3]_ptr", align 8
   %3 = fmul contract double %"a::Tuple[9]_ptr.unbox", %"b::Tuple[3]_ptr.unbox"
   %4 = fadd contract double %2, %3
   ret double %4
; └
}
```

Why is that first `%0 = fmul contract`?  That's the `*` I wrote — and it is unexpected that passing that result to `muladd` would induce a contract without `@fastmath`.

We noticed this because having that `*` marked `contract` means that LLVM rearranges this operation on my ARM64 CPU.  Specifically, `f3` gives different answers in a function than the same code does at top-level.

```Julia
julia> f3(smat, svec)
-0.0908304842736846

julia> (a, b) = (smat, svec);

julia> muladd(a[9], b[3], muladd(a[6], b[2], a[3] * b[1]))
-0.09083048427368462

julia> # it's actually doing this:
       muladd(a[9], b[3], muladd(a[3], b[1], a[6] * b[2]))
-0.0908304842736846
```

I see this behavior both on v1.10.5 and today's nightly (1.12.0-DEV.1209 @ 55c40ce52eb).

```
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.12.0-DEV.1209
Commit 55c40ce52eb (2024-09-16 17:25 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org release
Platform Info:
  OS: macOS (arm64-apple-darwin22.4.0)
  CPU: 8 × Apple M1 Pro
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LLVM: libLLVM-18.1.7 (ORCJIT, apple-m1)
Threads: 1 default, 0 interactive, 1 GC (on 6 virtual cores)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55785/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55785/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2357287898","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55785#issuecomment-2357287898","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55785","id":2357287898,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6MgVva","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-18T01:10:09Z","updated_at":"2024-09-18T01:10:09Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"That `contract` is being added *outside* of `muladd` is an issue, of course, and a correctness bug at that.

I know you know this, but it seems that a reminder might be in order. idea here is that `A^p` is evaluated as `A^Int(p)` if `isinteger(p)`, but `Int(p)` may error if `p` is large. In such cases, we may express the exponent as `p = m*q + r`, where `m` and `r` are small enough so that `Int(m)` and `Int(r)` succeed, and the operation becomes `(A^m)^q * A^r`. This is evaluated recursively by repeatedly dividing the exponent. 

After this, the following works:
```julia
julia> [1 1e-10; 0 1] ^ (1e20)
2×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.0  1.0e10
 0.0  1.0
```

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55300, but I have not carried out any error analysis to check the accuracy of results in general. Suggestions are welcome. In particular, I'm unsure how the rounding in `divrem` impacts the results if the exponents are powers","user":{"login":"jishnub","id":10461665,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNDYxNjY1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10461665?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub","html_url":"https://github.com/jishnub","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The idea here is that `A^p` is evaluated as `A^Int(p)` if `isinteger(p)`, but `Int(p)` may error if `p` is large. In such cases, we may express the exponent as `p = m*q + r`, where `m` and `r` are small enough so that `Int(m)` and `Int(r)` succeed, and the operation becomes `(A^m)^q * A^r`. This is evaluated recursively by repeatedly dividing the exponent. 

After this, the following works:
```julia
julia> [1 1e-10; 0 1] ^ (1e20)
2×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.0  1.0e10
 0.0  1.0
```

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55300, but I have not carried out any error analysis to check the accuracy of results in general. Suggestions are welcome. In particular, I'm unsure how the rounding in `divrem` impacts the results if the exponents are idea here is that `A^p` is evaluated as `A^Int(p)` if `isinteger(p)`, but `Int(p)` may error if `p` is large. In such cases, we may express the exponent as `p = m*q + r`, where `m` and `r` are small enough so that `Int(m)` and `Int(r)` succeed, and the operation becomes `(A^m)^q * A^r`. This is evaluated recursively by repeatedly dividing the exponent. 

After this, the following works:
```julia
julia> [1 1e-10; 0 1] ^ (1e20)
2×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.0  1.0e10
 0.0  1.0
```

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55300, but I have not carried out any error analysis to check the accuracy of results in general. Suggestions are welcome. In particular, I'm unsure how the rounding in `divrem` impacts the results if the exponents are powers","user":{"login":"jishnub","id":10461665,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNDYxNjY1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10461665?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub","html_url":"https://github.com/jishnub","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The idea here is that `A^p` is evaluated as `A^Int(p)` if `isinteger(p)`, but `Int(p)` may error if `p` is large. In such cases, we may express the exponent as `p = m*q + r`, where `m` and `r` are small enough so that `Int(m)` and `Int(r)` succeed, and the operation becomes `(A^m)^q * A^r`. This is evaluated recursively by repeatedly dividing the exponent. 

After this, the following works:
```julia
julia> [1 1e-10; 0 1] ^ (1e20)
2×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.0  1.0e10
 0.0  1.0
```

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55300, but I have not carried out any error analysis to check the accuracy of results in general. Suggestions are welcome. In particular, I'm unsure how the rounding in `divrem` impacts the results if the exponents are idea here is that `A^p` is evaluated as `A^Int(p)` if `isinteger(p)`, but `Int(p)` may error if `p` is large. In such cases, we may express the exponent as `p = m*q + r`, where `m` and `r` are small enough so that `Int(m)` and `Int(r)` succeed, and the operation becomes `(A^m)^q * A^r`. This is evaluated recursively by repeatedly dividing the exponent. 

After this, the following works:
```julia
julia> [1 1e-10; 0 1] ^ (1e20)
2×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.0  1.0e10
 0.0  1.0
```

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55300, but I have not carried out any error analysis to check the accuracy of results in general. Suggestions are welcome. In particular, I'm unsure how the rounding in `divrem` impacts the results if the exponents are powers","user":{"login":"jishnub","id":10461665,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNDYxNjY1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10461665?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub","html_url":"https://github.com/jishnub","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The idea here is that `A^p` is evaluated as `A^Int(p)` if `isinteger(p)`, but `Int(p)` may error if `p` is large. In such cases, we may express the exponent as `p = m*q + r`, where `m` and `r` are small enough so that `Int(m)` and `Int(r)` succeed, and the operation becomes `(A^m)^q * A^r`. This is evaluated recursively by repeatedly dividing the exponent. 

After this, the following works:
```julia
julia> [1 1e-10; 0 1] ^ (1e20)
2×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.0  1.0e10
 0.0  1.0
```

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55300, but I have not carried out any error analysis to check the accuracy of results in general. Suggestions are welcome. In particular, I'm unsure how the rounding in `divrem` impacts the results if the exponents are idea here is that `A^p` is evaluated as `A^Int(p)` if `isinteger(p)`, but `Int(p)` may error if `p` is large. In such cases, we may express the exponent as `p = m*q + r`, where `m` and `r` are small enough so that `Int(m)` and `Int(r)` succeed, and the operation becomes `(A^m)^q * A^r`. This is evaluated recursively by repeatedly dividing the exponent. 

After this, the following works:
```julia
julia> [1 1e-10; 0 1] ^ (1e20)
2×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.0  1.0e10
 0.0  1.0
```

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55300, but I have not carried out any error analysis to check the accuracy of results in general. Suggestions are welcome. In particular, I'm unsure how the rounding in `divrem` impacts the results if the exponents are powers","user":{"login":"jishnub","id":10461665,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNDYxNjY1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10461665?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub","html_url":"https://github.com/jishnub","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The idea here is that `A^p` is evaluated as `A^Int(p)` if `isinteger(p)`, but `Int(p)` may error if `p` is large. In such cases, we may express the exponent as `p = m*q + r`, where `m` and `r` are small enough so that `Int(m)` and `Int(r)` succeed, and the operation becomes `(A^m)^q * A^r`. This is evaluated recursively by repeatedly dividing the exponent. 

After this, the following works:
```julia
julia> [1 1e-10; 0 1] ^ (1e20)
2×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.0  1.0e10
 0.0  1.0
```

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55300, but I have not carried out any error analysis to check the accuracy of results in general. Suggestions are welcome. In particular, I'm unsure how the rounding in `divrem` impacts the results if the exponents are idea here is that `A^p` is evaluated as `A^Int(p)` if `isinteger(p)`, but `Int(p)` may error if `p` is large. In such cases, we may express the exponent as `p = m*q + r`, where `m` and `r` are small enough so that `Int(m)` and `Int(r)` succeed, and the operation becomes `(A^m)^q * A^r`. This is evaluated recursively by repeatedly dividing the exponent. 

After this, the following works:
```julia
julia> [1 1e-10; 0 1] ^ (1e20)
2×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.0  1.0e10
 0.0  1.0
```

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55300, but I have not carried out any error analysis to check the accuracy of results in general. Suggestions are welcome. In particular, I'm unsure how the rounding in `divrem` impacts the results if the exponents are powers","user":{"login":"jishnub","id":10461665,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNDYxNjY1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10461665?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub","html_url":"https://github.com/jishnub","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jishnub/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The idea here is that `A^p` is evaluated as `A^Int(p)` if `isinteger(p)`, but `Int(p)` may error if `p` is large. In such cases, we may express the exponent as `p = m*q + r`, where `m` and `r` are small enough so that `Int(m)` and `Int(r)` succeed, and the operation becomes `(A^m)^q * A^r`. This is evaluated recursively by repeatedly dividing the exponent. 

After this, the following works:
```julia
julia> [1 1e-10; 0 1] ^ (1e20)
2×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.0  1.0e10
 0.0  1.0
```

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55300, but I have not carried out any error analysis to check the accuracy of results in general. Suggestions are welcome. In particular, I'm unsure how the rounding in `divrem` impacts the results if the exponents are {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55488","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55488/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55488/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55488/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55488","id":2464261021,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6S4aOd","number":55488,"title":"Allow uplo='F' for Symmetric and Hermitian wrappers?","user":{"login":"amontoison","id":35051714,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM1MDUxNzE0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/35051714?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/amontoison","html_url":"https://github.com/amontoison","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amontoison/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amontoison/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amontoison/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amontoison/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amontoison/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amontoison/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amontoison/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amontoison/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amontoison/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-08-13T21:32:07Z","updated_at":"2024-08-14T15:11:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"It could help to determine whether a symmetric or Hermitian sparse matrix wrapped in `Symmetric` or `Hermitian` contains both triangles or not ('F' = full).

With sparse matrices on GPUs, both triangles are always needed for the routines provided by NVIDIA / AMD / Intel, and this check could be useful.
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55488/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55488/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2289079242","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55488#issuecomment-2289079242","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55488","id":2289079242,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6IcJPK","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-14T15:11:32Z","updated_at":"2024-08-14T15:11:32Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I worry about keeping the object valid under the `:F` mode. Sure, we could check it when it is wrapped, but if the parent is mutated asymmetrically then the object becomes invalid.
```julia-repl
julia> x = [1 2; 2 3]
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2
 2  3

julia> y = Hermitian(x, :U) # suppose we used :F here instead
2×2 Hermitian{Int64, Matrix{Int64}}:
 1  2
 2  3

julia> x[2] = 3; parent(y) # y would be silently invalidated if mode :F
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2
 3  3
```

I think you'd get the same benefit from passing the matrix (with or without a wrapper) with a `checkherm=false` keyword to skip a `ishermitian` validation within the target function. That shifts responsibility for the validity of the object from the wrapper (who cannot hope to ensure its integrity short of the performance nonstarter of checking itself at every use) to the caller.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2289079242/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/37977","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/37977/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/37977/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/37977/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/37977","id":718675372,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU3MTg2NzUzNzI=","number":37977,"title":"Fix pi == one(pi)*pi or update docs for one()","user":{"login":"petvana","id":8127727,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgxMjc3Mjc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8127727?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/petvana","html_url":"https://github.com/petvana","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/petvana/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/petvana/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/petvana/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/petvana/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/petvana/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/petvana/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/petvana/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/petvana/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/petvana/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":171885699,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNzE4ODU2OTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/domain:maths","name":"domain:maths","color":"93755D","default":false,"description":"Mathematical functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":18,"created_at":"2020-10-10T18:24:23Z","updated_at":"2024-08-26T15:58:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Based on the discussion in #37931, I have found that `pi == one(pi)*pi` is broken. However, it should hold according to the documentation of `one()` function:

``` julia
    one(x)
    one(T::type)
Return a multiplicative identity for `x`: a value such that
`one(x)*x == x*one(x) == x`.  Alternatively `one(T)` can
take a type `T`, in which case `one` returns a multiplicative
identity for any `x` of type `T`.

If possible, `one(x)` returns a value of the same type as `x`,
and `one(T)` returns a value of type `T`.  However, this may
not be the case for types representing dimensionful quantities
(e.g. time in days), since the multiplicative
identity must be dimensionless.  In that case, `one(x)`
should return an identity value of the same precision
(and shape, for matrices) as `x`.
...
```

Don't know if you prefer fixing the implementation or just updating the documentation to make it consistent. Notice that since `one(pi)` already returns `true`, it would be possible to make `true * pi === pi`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/37977/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/37977/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2310543690","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/37977#issuecomment-2310543690","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/37977","id":2310543690,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6JuBlK","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-26T15:58:04Z","updated_at":"2024-08-26T15:58:04Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"It seems that `Irrational` is basically `AbstractFloat` with infinity digits. The difference is that, whereas `promote` usually takes two arguments and makes them the wider type, instead it demotes `Irrational` to the (`float` of) the other argument. In this sense, `Irrational` has its uses (but `pi(::Type)` might have been the better choice).

So one candidate resolution (which I will dismiss in my next paragraph) is to change `==` for `Irrational` to promote the arguments first (although `Integer` comparisons could virtually always be false), so that `pi == x` if `oftype(x, pi) == x`.

The problem is that this would create an inconsistency with inequalities. Because `Float64(pi) < pi` is true and this is "more true" than them being `==`. So the implementation of a `promote`-based comparison would create a violation of the incredibly useful and widely-assumed property that two reals (except `NaN`) should be related by precisely one of `<`, `==`, or `>`. 

Maybe this is the original sin of the `Irrational` concept? We have other issues, like the current "paradox" that `pi - Float64(pi) == 0.0` even though `pi > Float64(pi)` and the difference would be easily approximated as another `Float64`.

All of this is easily reconciled by the trivial fact that `Irrational` does not propagate through virtually any non-symbolic calculation. Existential issues with `Irrational` aside, I'm not really worried about this issue. `T(pi) == one(T(pi)) * T(pi)` is true for any `T<:AbstractFloat` and I think that's good enough for me. Where in practice does one check an (uncasted) `Irrational` in a `==` with an arithmetic result?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2310543690/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

etc."}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":18,"created_at":"2024-04-03T22:10:38Z","updated_at":"2024-08-30T14:51:12Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53945","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53945","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53945.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53945.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"OK, here's take two, building on the work that was done in #53871.  We preserve the neutral element requirement but guarantee that `init` is used to start all evaluation chains.

The news summarizes as succinctly as I can:

> `init` is now guaranteed to be used for non-empty collections one or more times. This ensures that all calls
  to the reducing function `op` have one argument that is either `init` or the result from a
  previous `op` evaluation. Previously `init` was explicitly allowed to be omitted from the reduction entirely.

And the docs for `mapreduce` say a bit more:

> If provided, `init` is the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` have one argument that is either `init`
or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
unspecified. As it may appear in the reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that
does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.

Closes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49042.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53945/reactions","total_count":5,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":4,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53945/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2321506784","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53945#issuecomment-2321506784","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53945","id":2321506784,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6KX2Hg","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-30T14:51:10Z","updated_at":"2024-08-30T14:51:10Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"One behavior I'd like to preserve is
```julia-repl
julia> sum(fill(-0.0, 0))
0.0

julia> sum(fill(-0.0, 3))
-0.0
```
It seems that the way to get this is to make `sum` on `IEEEFloat` use `init=-0.0` (the `IEEEFloat` additive identity) but allow (the option of) a distinct return value of `+0.0` for the `isempty` case. I hate that this makes the implementation more complicated but I don't see a great way around it.

I would be somewhat unhappy if a sum of `-0.0`s yielded `+0.0` (`sum(x)` would differ from `+(x...)` in a not-just-reassociation way), and I expect the change would break things (both existing and yet-unwritten) in at least a few places, but I know most people would be unhappy if an empty sum yielded `-0.0` (and this would almost certainly break things in many places).

(`+0.0` vs `-0.0` might seem pretty inconsequential until you write `inv(sum(x))`)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2321506784/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I guess this could matter if the function was inlined into a type-unstable context, but that nuance is not explained here. And it would seem that ["Annotate values taken from untyped locations"](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/performance-tips/#Annotate-values-taken-from-untyped-locations) would mitigate this issue in most practical contexts, anyway.

We already have users C-style concrete-annotating every function argument while thinking they're doing themselves and others a favor. I'd rather they not get inspired (and encouraged... if only through their misinterpretation) to litter function bodies with `(x isa Integer) || error("not integer")` when the compiler usually knows the concrete type already.

I think this example should use a concrete type like `Int`. Abstract type annotations seldom do much to remove the impact of poor inference so I'd rather not imply that they are ever part of "performance tips compliant" code.

---

Given the complex understanding of code context and the intersection of concepts necessary to even appreciate the tiny slice of situations where this would actually apply (and to understand the overwhelming majority of cases where it does *not*), I think I would prefer not to put this into the performance tips. I would rather field the rare resulting issue on Discourse, where someone can hopefully point to (or rediscover) #38274. As a performance tip, I think it would mostly be misunderstood and applied when inapplicable. I think it risks more harm than good. 

It appears undisputed that the linked issue is something that "should" get fixed, so this performance tip might expire anyway. If the issue hadn't been open for four years already, I would fight that point harder.

Or do I grossly underestimate or misunderstand how often this is relevant, even after "Annotate values taken from untyped locations" is followed?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2228681859/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I am trying to do the Cholesky decomposition of the following Gram matrix:

$$
G = U^TU
$$

where:
 
$$
U = \begin{pmatrix}
64337 & 17852\\
8480 & 2353
\end{pmatrix}
$$


$G$ is positive definite by Gram matrix definition.

## How to reproduce

```
using LinearAlgebra

U = stack([[64337, 8480], [17852, 2353]])

cholesky(U' * U)
``` 

It return a `PosDefExecption` exception:
```
PosDefException: matrix is not positive definite; Cholesky factorization failed.
```

I think is related to numerical issues, since the eigenvalues of the matrix $G$ are:

$$
(2.1233931835062947\text{e-8}, 4.535390482\text{e9})
$$

So very close to 0.

Info:

Julia Version 1.10.4
Commit 48d4fd48430 (2024-06-04 10:41 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: macOS (arm64-apple-darwin22.4.0)
  CPU: 10 × Apple M1 Pro
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-15.0.7 (ORCJIT, apple-m1)
Threads: 8 default, 0 interactive, 4 GC (on 8 virtual cores)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55126/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55126/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2228983724","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55126#issuecomment-2228983724","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55126","id":2228983724,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6E25es","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-15T17:02:33Z","updated_at":"2024-07-15T17:02:33Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"As remarked above, the issue is that the matrix is very ill-conditioned. There isn't a lot that can be done, in general, to address this, short of using more precision (like `BigFloat` in the above suggestion)

If you truly have the $G = U^\top U$ construction in your situation, consider using the QR decomposition `Q,R = qr(U)`, which will yield $G = R^\top Q^\top Q R = R^\top R = L L^\top$. You might need to flip the sign of some rows of $R$ to make the diagonal positive like in a proper Cholesky decomposition (e.g., `R = sign.(Diagonal(R)) * R`)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2228983724/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
