I agree that `axes` and `XXXindex` should use the `Int` interface, as these are "owned" by the `AbstractArray` interface. These would match `Tuple`.

I would argue that a `NamedTuple` is an (ordered) dict-like construct. Why else use a `NamedTuple` if not to use the names? If anything, I consider `Int` indexing to be the secondary mode. Thus, I would argue that `keys` and `pairs` should continue to use the `Symbol` keys. Why even have `keys`/`eachindex` be distinct functions unless they can occasionally disagree? Also, I think `pairs` with `Symbol` is currently baked into splatted/slurped keyword argument handling (which also supports `Dict`), to say nothing of package and user code.

We can add `axes` support since it's currently a `MethodError`. `eachindex` could be changed to return a `Base.OneTo` to match `Tuple`, although this might result in a few breakages across the ecosystem (and may be too breaking pre-v2.0). I would leave the other functions we've discussed as they are.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1559904950/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49932","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49932/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49932/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49932/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49932","id":1720947626,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5mk5Oq","number":49932,"title":"NamedTuple `firstindex` and `lastindex` do not match `eachindex`","user":{"login":"adienes","id":51664769,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjUxNjY0NzY5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/51664769?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/adienes","html_url":"https://github.com/adienes","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adienes/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adienes/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adienes/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adienes/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adienes/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adienes/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adienes/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adienes/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adienes/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2023-05-23T01:00:45Z","updated_at":"2023-05-23T18:33:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"```
julia> x = (; a=1, b=2)
(a = 1, b = 2)

julia> firstindex(x)
1

julia> lastindex(x)
2

julia> eachindex(x)
(:a, :b)
```

but actually in most places, `NamedTuple` cannot be indexed "array-like" in the same way that `Tuple` can
```
julia> x[begin]
1

julia> x[end]
2

julia> x[begin:end]
ERROR: MethodError

julia> x[:]
ERROR: MethodError

julia> x[[true, true]]
ERROR: MethodError
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49932/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49932/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1559949479","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49932#issuecomment-1559949479","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49932","id":1559949479,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5c-vCn","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-05-23T18:33:30Z","updated_at":"2023-05-23T18:33:30Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> In a future version, I would rather separate the concepts cleanly:
> 
>     * `getproperty(::NamedTuple, ::Symbol)`
> 
>     * `getindex(::NamedTuple, ::Int)`
> 
> 
> Any API that mixes these together in one function is bound to cause confusion.

But an API that doesn't support `getindex(::NamedTuple,::Symbol)` cannot be used interchangeably with one that also works for `Dict{Symbol,<:Any}`. I don't see a problem with allowing them to permit a common interface. Further, vararg keyword arguments are sometimes a `Dict` and sometimes a `NamedTuple` so it's necessary to have some way to handle them uniformly.

---

> ```
> julia> x = (; a=1, b=2)
> (a = 1, b = 2)
> 
> julia> haskey(x, 1)
> true
> 
> julia> 1 in keys(x)
> false
> ```

I'm sympathetic to the disagreement here. However, I would argue that `haskey` is the idiomatic version of this and `k in keys(x)` is less idiomatic and may not be expected to be correct in all cases. In either case, one would always have `first(keys(x)) in keys(x)`. I would compare this to the hazards of `for i in 1:length(x)`.

In some sense, you are right to argue (perhaps facetiously) that `keys` should perhaps return both the indices and names if both are permitted. But I expect we'll both agree that in practice this would make it largely unusable as one could not iterate the set without iterating the `NamedTuple` twice.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1559949479/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

#49947 by implementing `allassigned

We can have a fast path for bits eltypes ant bits union eltypes where the storage device is an `Array`. The remaining cases can often just unwrap the wrapper array and depend on eventually hitting this.

* `Bidiagonal`
* `SymTridiagonal`
* `Tridiagonal`
* `Adjoint`
* `Transpose`
* `Diagonal`
* `LinearIndices`
* `AbstractRange`
* `PermutedDimsArray`
* `CartesianIndices`

The following only have placeholders implemented because I still need to give some more thought into how to do this efficiently where we want to know if the underlying array has a fast path to checking if all are assigned before taking the time of checking the subset of indices represented by the wrapping array.

* `UpperHessenberg`
* `Symmetric`
* `Hermitian`
* `AbstractTriangular`
* `SubArray`


","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49956/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49956/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1564603777","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49956#issuecomment-1564603777","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49956","id":1564603777,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5dQfWB","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-05-26T15:59:14Z","updated_at":"2023-05-26T15:59:14Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Checks like your `allassigned(V::SubArray) = allassigned(V.parent)` will return the wrong result if the entire `SubArray` is `isassigned` but the larger `parent` array contains unassigned entries.

I'm sure I don't understand all the nuance you're after in this PR, but I notice this:
```julia-repl
julia> using BenchmarkTools

julia> x = Array{Int}(undef,100);

julia> y = fill(x,100);

julia> @btime all(i->(isassigned($x,i)),eachindex($x))
  33.333 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
true

julia> @btime all(i->@inbounds(isassigned($x,i)),eachindex($x))
  2.300 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
true

julia> @btime all(i->(isassigned($y,i)),eachindex($y))
  42.020 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
true

julia> @btime all(i->@inbounds(isassigned($y,i)),eachindex($y))
  41.818 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
true
```
Marking the `isassigned` with `@inbounds` makes the type-determinable case free. In other words, checks on arrays that can never contain `#undef` are elided entirely once the `@boundscheck` is removed.

It seems that extending `isassigned(V,I...)` to accept the same sorts of `I...` as `getindex` and works through the native indexing machinery (including not accessing masked parts of arrays wrapped by `SubArray`, `Hermitian`, etc) would be both simpler, extend to new types, and would avoid the need for a new function. A method like
```julia
Base.@propagate_inbounds function isassigned(a, I...)
	@boundscheck checkbounds(Bool, a, I...) || return false
	# iterate over the requested indices and check `@inbounds isassigned`
end
```
would permit your targeted functionality to be achieved as `isassigned(A, :)`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1564603777/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49405/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49405/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1567231420","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49405#issuecomment-1567231420","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49405","id":1567231420,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5dag28","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-05-29T14:47:01Z","updated_at":"2023-05-29T14:47:01Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Personally, I still don't think the decision to `contract` should be coupled into `@simd`. I'll reiterate my pitch and then shut up about it, as this PR is still a huge improvement over the current behavior.

`contract` is probably the most boring fastmath flag as the best it can ever do is turn 2 instructions into 1. It can never enable SIMD that was otherwise impossible (except maybe in an edge case where it dodges a register spill?) or eliminate a code block. The fact that `contract` couldn't accelerate a dot product in the above benchmarks (which was a surprise to me and could change in a different benchmark) further diminishes the performance arguments for it.

Meanwhile, `contract` is the only flag that can currently be enabled independently. `muladd` is easy to add manually, `@fastmath` is even easier (if one can suffer the extra flags or if we get #49890), and both can be used in combination with `@simd`. But there is still is no way (or plan for a way) to *disable* flags.

There will absolutely be cases (although not *many* cases) where someone requires that operations do not `contract`. Complex multiplication comes to mind, where implementing it with `contract` can cause `imag(x*x') != 0`.

P.S.
Maybe this should get a news item? Some people may observe performance regressions. It would be nice (and may avoid some bug reports) to note this change and suggest that the previous behavior and performance can be recovered via `@fastmath @simd`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1567231420/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372","id":1669479222,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5jgjs2","number":49372,"title":"Should `allequal` use `==` instead of `isequal`?","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":2988687317,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyOTg4Njg3MzE3","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/equality","name":"equality","color":"5080DD","default":false,"description":"Issues relating to equality relations: ==, ===, isequal"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2023-04-15T16:42:57Z","updated_at":"2023-04-17T14:44:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"When I worked on the PR to add `allequal` (#43353), I used `isequal` instead of `==`, because `unique` and `allunique` use `isequal`. However, in retrospect that seems like the wrong choice. It seems to me that users looking for `allequal` functionality will generally want to compare values with `==`. In particular, it is unfortunate that `allequal` does not follow three-valued logic. So, we currently have the following behavior, which seems undesirable to me:

```julia
julia> allequal([NaN, NaN])
true

julia> allequal([missing, missing])
true

julia> allequal([1, missing])
false
```

I think for most use cases it would be better for those to return `false`, `missing`, and `missing`, respectively.

In the PR discussion, I [mentioned the difference](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43354#issuecomment-988359411) between using `isequal` and using `==`, but nobody objected to using `isequal`. 😅 

Unfortunately this would be a breaking change. I would almost consider the failure to propagate missingness a bug, but technically it's not a bug since the docstring does say that comparisons are made using `isequal`.

Would this qualify as one of those "technically breaking" changes that we could slip into a minor release?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1511507833","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372#issuecomment-1511507833","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372","id":1511507833,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5aF8d5","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-04-17T14:44:24Z","updated_at":"2023-04-17T14:44:24Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Would there be room for keyword arguments like our sorting functions have? Reduced example derived from an effort to write a faster `norm(x, 2)`:
```julia
# Julia v1.8.0

function test(x)
	scale = exp2(768)
	s1 = 0.0
	s2 = 0.0
	@simd for z in x
		s1 += abs2(z)
		s2 += abs2(z*scale)
	end
	s = s2==Inf ? s1 : s2
	return s,s1,s2
end

test(1:3)
# (Inf, 14.0, Inf)
```

Inspecting the `@code_llvm` shows many `@fastmath` operations and the `@code_native` shows unauthorized `fma` instructions. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49422","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49422/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49422/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49422/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49422","id":1675328642,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5j23yC","number":49422,"title":"You can convert infinity to an integer (Float16)","user":{"login":"yurivish","id":114388,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNDM4OA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/114388?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish","html_url":"https://github.com/yurivish","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":97636,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5NzYzNg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"e10c02","default":true,"description":"Indicates an unexpected problem or unintended behavior"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-04-19T17:29:40Z","updated_at":"2023-04-20T17:12:52Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Julia’s [`convert`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/base/#Base.convert) function is designed to let users safely convert a floating-point integers such as `2.0` into the equivalent `Int` value, such as `2`. It is documented to throw an error if given a non-integer value:

> If `T` is an `Integer` type, an `InexactError` will be raised if `x` is not representable by `T`, for example if `x` is not integer-valued, or is outside the range supported by `T`.

However, it will silently convert infinity into an integer if you give it `-Inf16`:

```julia
julia> isinteger(-Inf16) # infinity is not an integer
false

julia> convert(Int, -Inf16) # but Julia 1.6 – 1.9rc2 will happily turn it into one
-9223372036854775808
```

This behavior only occurs for 16-bit negative infinity:

```julia
julia> convert(Int, -Inf32)
ERROR: InexactError: Int64(-Inf)

julia> convert(Int, -Inf64)
ERROR: InexactError: Int64(-Inf)
```

You can convert infinity into an `Int32`, `Int64`, or `Int128` and it will take on a different integer value for each:

```julia
julia> Int32(-Inf16)
-2147483648

julia> Int64(-Inf16)
-9223372036854775808

julia> Int128(-Inf16)
0
```

I debugged this and found that it happens because `convert(Int, x)` calls `Int(x)`, which has an bug that causes it to mess up the [validity check](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/17cfb8e65ead377bf1b4598d8a9869144142c84e/base/float.jl#L763) for `-Inf16`:

```julia
function (::Type{$Ti})(x::$Tf)
    if ($(Tf(typemin(Ti))) <= x <= $(Tf(typemax(Ti)))) && (round(x, RoundToZero) == x)
        return unsafe_trunc($Ti,x)
    else
        throw(InexactError($(Expr(:quote,Ti.name.name)), $Ti, x))
    end
end
```

The comparison on the second line produces the incorrect behavior, because `Float16(typemin(Int))` == `-Inf16`:

```julia
julia> Float16(typemin(Int64))
-Inf16

julia> Float32(typemin(Int64))
-9.223372f18

julia> Float64(typemin(Int64))
-9.223372036854776e18
```

Which causes the interval check to succeed:

```julia
Float16(typemin(Int)) <= x < Float16(typemax(Int)))
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49450","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49450/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49450/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49450/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49450","id":1677593633,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5j_gwh","number":49450,"title":"Incorrect results from floating-point division (`fld`, `cld`, `div`)","user":{"login":"yurivish","id":114388,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNDM4OA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/114388?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish","html_url":"https://github.com/yurivish","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yurivish/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":97636,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5NzYzNg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"e10c02","default":true,"description":"Indicates an unexpected problem or unintended behavior"},{"id":171885699,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNzE4ODU2OTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/maths","name":"maths","color":"009800","default":false,"description":"Mathematical functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2023-04-21T00:38:48Z","updated_at":"2023-04-21T15:57:49Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"
Julia has a [`div`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/math/#Base.div) function that is used to implement [floor division](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/math/#Base.fld) (`fld`) and [ceil division](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/math/#Base.cld) (`cld`). I think I’ve found a bug that causes all of these division functions to return incorrect results.

Floor division is documented to return the largest integer less than or equal to `x / y`. It should never return a number that is *greater* than `x / y`.

But it does:

```julia
julia> 514 / Float16(0.75)
Float16(685.5)

julia> div(514, Float16(0.75)) # should round down, but rounds up instead
Float16(686.0)

julia> fld(514, Float16(0.75)) # likewise
Float16(686.0)

julia> fld(514, Float16(0.75)) ≤ 514 / Float16(0.75)
false
```

Similarly, ceil division should never return a number that is *smaller* than regular division, but it does:

```julia
julia> 515 / Float16(0.75)
Float16(686.5)

julia> cld(515, Float16(0.75)) # should round up, but rounds down instead
Float16(686.0)

julia> cld(515, Float16(0.75)) ≥ 515 / Float16(0.75)
false
```

This behavior is not limited to 16-bit floats. Here’s a case where `fld` produces an incorrect result for `Float32` inputs:

```julia
julia> 4_194_307 / Float32(0.75) # = 5592409.5
5.5924095f6

julia> fld(4_194_307, Float32(0.75)) # = 5592410, incorrectly rounded up
5.59241f6

julia> fld(4_194_307, Float32(0.75)) ≤ 4_194_307 / Float32(0.75)
false
```

And here’s the same for `cld`:

```julia
julia> 4_194_308 / Float32(0.75) # = 5592410.5

julia> cld(4_194_308, Float32(0.75)) # = 5592410, incorrectly rounded down
5.59241f6

julia> cld(4_194_308, Float32(0.75)) ≥ 4_194_308 / Float32(0.75)
false
```

The equivalent operations in Python produce the correct results:

```python
# For 16-bit floats:
>>> np.float16(514) / np.float16(0.75) # regular division
685.5
>>> np.float16(514) // np.float16(0.75) # floor division
685.0

# For 32-bit floats:
>>> np.float32(4_194_307) / np.float32(0.75) # regular division
5592409.5
>>> np.float32(4_194_307) // np.float32(0.75) # floor division
5592409.0
```

Examples of this incorrect behavior are not hard to find – for most floats, you can find a divisor that will make either `fld` or `cld` return the wrong answer.

Here are some examples for `Float16` where either `fld` or `cld` is incorrect:

- `cld(1, Float16(0.000999)) < 1 / Float16(0.000999)`
- `cld(2, Float16(0.001999)) < 2 / Float16(0.001999)`
- `cld(3, Float16(0.002934)) < 3 / Float16(0.002934)`
- `cld(4, Float16(0.003998)) < 4 / Float16(0.003998)`
- `fld(5, Float16(0.004925)) > 5 / Float16(0.004925)`

And here are some for `Float32`:

- `fld(5, Float32(6.556511e-7)) > 5 / Float32(6.556511e-7)`
- `fld(10, Float32(1.3113022e-6)) > 10 / Float32(1.3113022e-6)`
- `fld(11, Float32(1.4305115e-6)) > 11 / Float32(1.4305115e-6)`
- `cld(16, Float32(2.8014183e-6)) < 16 / Float32(2.8014183e-6)`
- `cld(17, Float32(2.2053719e-6)) < 17 / Float32(2.2053719e-6)`

For simplicity I’ve presented examples where the first argument is an integer; this bug also occurs for non-integral inputs.

A divisor producing the wrong result can be found for over 51% of all possible 16-bit floats. I have not evaluated how widespread this is for `Float32`, but the results above suggest that it is similarly easy to find failures there too.

I’ve tracked the invocations down to [this definition](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/72aec423c2ab9f80c249d63fdd68b35833cfd7ed/base/div.jl#L370) of `div`, which has existed at least as far back as [Julia 1.6](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/f9720dc2ebd6cd9e3086365f281e62506444ef37/base/div.jl#L279):

```julia
div(x::T, y::T, r::RoundingMode) where {T<:AbstractFloat} =
	convert(T, round((x - rem(x, y, r)) / y))
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49450/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49450/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1518033890","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49450#issuecomment-1518033890","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49450","id":1518033890,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5ae1vi","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-04-21T15:57:49Z","updated_at":"2023-04-21T15:57:49Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"# The problem

Here's what I found digging into this:
```julia-repl
julia> x,y,r = Float16(514),Float16(0.75),RoundDown
(Float16(514.0), Float16(0.75), RoundingMode{:Down}())

julia> divrem(widen(x),y,r)
(685.0f0, 0.25f0)

julia> divrem(x,y,r) # div is wrong, but rem is correct
(Float16(686.0), Float16(0.25))

julia> widen(x)/y
685.3333f0

julia> x/y
Float16(685.5)

julia> # div(x::T, y::T, r::RoundingMode) where {T<:AbstractFloat} = convert(T,round((x-rem(x,y,r))/y))
       div(x,y,r)
Float16(686.0)

julia> eps(x)
Float16(0.5)

julia> x-rem(x,y,r) # == x due to limited precision
Float16(514.0)

julia> (x-rem(x,y,r)) / y
Float16(685.5)

julia> # note: applying rounding mode, relative to implementation
       # would be correct in this case, but I don't know whether to trust it in general
       round((x-rem(x,y,r)) / y, r)
Float16(685.0)
```

So the issue appears to be with the implementation
```julia
div(x::T, y::T, r::RoundingMode) where {T<:AbstractFloat} = convert(T,round((x-rem(x,y,r))/y))
```
It assumes that `x-rem(x,y,r)` will be roughly evenly divisible by `y`. This is only true if there is small roundoff error in the subtraction. It then rounds the number to the nearest integer (which would address small errors in the subtraction, but not large ones). This means that, in the worst case of total underflow during the subtraction (as we see in the example), we effectively get `div(x,y,::RoundingMode) = round(x/y)`. One notices that the rounding mode is effectively "unused" when the remainder is small relative to the numerator.

These examples show that this manifests when `x - rem(x,y,r) == x` and the floating point rounding mode (usually `RoundNearest` (ties go to even) resolves a `0.5` fractional part of `x/y` in the "unlucky" direction.

Also, with this failure mode in mind, it's entirely possible to construct failing examples in any precision:
```julia-repl
julia> x,y,r = 1e0,eps(x)*(3//2),RoundUp
(1.0, 3.3306690738754696e-16, RoundingMode{:Up}())

julia> div(x,y,RoundDown) <= x/y, div(x,y,RoundUp) >= x/y
(true, false)
```
Note that `BigFloat` has its own method and does not suffer from this bug.

# A step in the right direction

It appears that redefining
```julia
newdiv(x::T, y::T, r::RoundingMode) where {T<:AbstractFloat} = convert(T,round((x-rem(x,y,r))/y,r)) # add rounding mode to `round` call

function count_wrong_floats()
    count_old_wrong = 0
    count_new_wrong = 0
    for i in 0x0000:0xffff
        x = reinterpret(Float16, i)
        for j in 0x0000:0xffff
            y = reinterpret(Float16, j)
            quotient = x / y
            old_is_wrong = (div(x, y, RoundDown) >  quotient) | (div(x, y, RoundUp) < quotient)
            new_is_wrong = (newdiv(x, y, RoundDown) >  quotient) | (newdiv(x, y, RoundUp) < quotient)
            count_old_wrong += old_is_wrong
            count_new_wrong += new_is_wrong
        end
    end
    return count_old_wrong,count_new_wrong
end

count_wrong_floats()
# (2511024, 2792)
```
has significantly reduced the number of incorrect input pairs. I'm out of time to chase down the remaining issues right now.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1518033890/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2023-04-21T00:38:48Z","updated_at":"2023-04-21T18:36:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"
Julia has a [`div`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/math/#Base.div) function that is used to implement [floor division](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/math/#Base.fld) (`fld`) and [ceil division](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/math/#Base.cld) (`cld`). I think I’ve found a bug that causes all of these division functions to return incorrect results.

Floor division is documented to return the largest integer less than or equal to `x / y`. It should never return a number that is *greater* than `x / y`.

But it does:

```julia
julia> 514 / Float16(0.75)
Float16(685.5)

julia> div(514, Float16(0.75)) # should round down, but rounds up instead
Float16(686.0)

julia> fld(514, Float16(0.75)) # likewise
Float16(686.0)

julia> fld(514, Float16(0.75)) ≤ 514 / Float16(0.75)
false
```

Similarly, ceil division should never return a number that is *smaller* than regular division, but it does:

```julia
julia> 515 / Float16(0.75)
Float16(686.5)

julia> cld(515, Float16(0.75)) # should round up, but rounds down instead
Float16(686.0)

julia> cld(515, Float16(0.75)) ≥ 515 / Float16(0.75)
false
```

This behavior is not limited to 16-bit floats. Here’s a case where `fld` produces an incorrect result for `Float32` inputs:

```julia
julia> 4_194_307 / Float32(0.75) # = 5592409.5
5.5924095f6

julia> fld(4_194_307, Float32(0.75)) # = 5592410, incorrectly rounded up
5.59241f6

julia> fld(4_194_307, Float32(0.75)) ≤ 4_194_307 / Float32(0.75)
false
```

And here’s the same for `cld`:

```julia
julia> 4_194_308 / Float32(0.75) # = 5592410.5

julia> cld(4_194_308, Float32(0.75)) # = 5592410, incorrectly rounded down
5.59241f6

julia> cld(4_194_308, Float32(0.75)) ≥ 4_194_308 / Float32(0.75)
false
```

The equivalent operations in Python produce the correct results:

```python
# For 16-bit floats:
>>> np.float16(514) / np.float16(0.75) # regular division
685.5
>>> np.float16(514) // np.float16(0.75) # floor division
685.0

# For 32-bit floats:
>>> np.float32(4_194_307) / np.float32(0.75) # regular division
5592409.5
>>> np.float32(4_194_307) // np.float32(0.75) # floor division
5592409.0
```

Examples of this incorrect behavior are not hard to find – for most floats, you can find a divisor that will make either `fld` or `cld` return the wrong answer.

Here are some examples for `Float16` where either `fld` or `cld` is incorrect:

- `cld(1, Float16(0.000999)) < 1 / Float16(0.000999)`
- `cld(2, Float16(0.001999)) < 2 / Float16(0.001999)`
- `cld(3, Float16(0.002934)) < 3 / Float16(0.002934)`
- `cld(4, Float16(0.003998)) < 4 / Float16(0.003998)`
- `fld(5, Float16(0.004925)) > 5 / Float16(0.004925)`

And here are some for `Float32`:

- `fld(5, Float32(6.556511e-7)) > 5 / Float32(6.556511e-7)`
- `fld(10, Float32(1.3113022e-6)) > 10 / Float32(1.3113022e-6)`
- `fld(11, Float32(1.4305115e-6)) > 11 / Float32(1.4305115e-6)`
- `cld(16, Float32(2.8014183e-6)) < 16 / Float32(2.8014183e-6)`
- `cld(17, Float32(2.2053719e-6)) < 17 / Float32(2.2053719e-6)`

For simplicity I’ve presented examples where the first argument is an integer; this bug also occurs for non-integral inputs.

A divisor producing the wrong result can be found for over 51% of all possible 16-bit floats. I have not evaluated how widespread this is for `Float32`, but the results above suggest that it is similarly easy to find failures there too.

I’ve tracked the invocations down to [this definition](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/72aec423c2ab9f80c249d63fdd68b35833cfd7ed/base/div.jl#L370) of `div`, which has existed at least as far back as [Julia 1.6](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/f9720dc2ebd6cd9e3086365f281e62506444ef37/base/div.jl#L279):

```julia
div(x::T, y::T, r::RoundingMode) where {T<:AbstractFloat} =
	convert(T, round((x - rem(x, y, r)) / y))
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49450/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49450/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1518202863","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49450#issuecomment-1518202863","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49450","id":1518202863,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5afe_v","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-04-21T18:36:05Z","updated_at":"2023-04-21T18:36:05Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> Which version are you on?

I added versioninfo to my earlier post. It's v1.8.0 (kinda old) on Intel x86-64 (non-native Float16). I ran the test again in a fresh session and reproduced my earlier number of failures. I'll remark that my number of resolved failures `2511024 - 2792` equals your number of resolved failures `2508232`, so it appears some orthogonal issue is resolved by a post-1.8.0 version or your hardware.

It's interesting to hear that some `Float32` examples were not resolved by this. {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48487","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48487/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48487/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48487/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48487","id":1566439817,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5dXfmJ","number":48487,"title":"Optmize float `min`/`max` with constant propagation","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-02-01T16:37:49Z","updated_at":"2023-02-01T16:37:49Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"For any `y` except `-0.0`, or `NaN`, it holds that `min(x,y) === min(y,x) === ifelse(y<x,y,x)`.
For any `y` except `+0.0`, or `NaN`, it holds that `min(x,y) === min(y,x) === ifelse(y<=x,y,x)`.
For any `y` except `-0.0`, or `NaN`, it holds that `max(x,y) === max(y,x) === ifelse(y>=x,y,x)`.
For any `y` except `+0.0`, or `NaN`, it holds that `max(x,y) === max(y,x) === ifelse(y>x,y,x)`.
For `isnan(y)` it holds that `isequal(min(x,y),y)` and `isequal(max(x,y),y)`. Note the lack of guaranteed `===` (except when `x` is not-NaN) so this may not be a desirable substitution.

In other words, when any `min`/`max` operation involves a known value (or a value known *not* be be one of a small set of values), it is possible to use the ternary definitions of these functions. When an argument is known `NaN` (not common in practice), one can skip the operation altogether at the risk of utilizing a different `NaN`. On x86, the ternary versions require just 1 instruction (`<` or `>`) or 2 instructions (`<=` or `>=`) while the general versions require roughly 8 (see #41709). As of #47814, aarch64 does not suffer this drawback as it has native support for our desired `min`/`max` semantics.

This means that common operations such as `max(x,c)` can be made much faster (on x86) than they currently are when `c` is known (for example, a compile-time constant). However, on aarch64 we might want to avoid this substitution as it might not be better than the natively supported general version.

One can make these substitutions manually, but it'd be pretty slick if the compiler could do this automatically. I don't know what it takes to impart this sort of optimization. This might be LLVM territory? I see this as similar to `sqrt(abs(x))` eliding the check-and-branch for negative arguments to `sqrt`, but that similarity may be superficial.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48487/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48487/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

can be seen in shortened `code_native(isfinite,Tuple{Float64})`. Checking for `NaN` is easier than checking for zero and those are the only two possibilities for `x - x`.
```julia-repl
julia> using BenchmarkTools

julia> x = rand(100);

julia> @btime all(isfinite,$x); # before
  58.350 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

julia> @btime all(isfinite,$x); # after
  47.166 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-02-04T16:58:58Z","updated_at":"2023-02-06T17:00:21Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"There are a bunch of different NaN values. `reinterpret(Float64, reinterpret(UInt64, NaN) + 1)` and `NaN` are two examples.

NaNs propagate through floating point operations. `sin(NaN)` must be `NaN` in the sense of `isnan(sin(NaN))`, but which NaN should it return? Must it return the canonical `NaN`? Must it return its input? May it return some other number that `isnan` for performance reasons? These questions come up for most math functions, `min`/`max`/`sort`, and possibly others.

I propose to explicitly document that mathematical functions (e.g. `sin`, `hypot`, `min`) will produce an NaN result on NaN input but that which NaN is produced is an implementation detail.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48523/reactions","total_count":4,"+1":4,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48523/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1419414441","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48523#issuecomment-1419414441","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48523","id":1419414441,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5Umoup","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-02-06T17:00:20Z","updated_at":"2023-02-06T17:00:20Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"In defiance of "never say never", it's not a horrible bet that *literally no Julia user* relies on the NaN semantics of any function beyond `isnan(f(NaN))`. For how exceedingly rarely somebody cares and how relatively cheaply one can wrap a function for a specific semantic, I think we can afford to adopt any-NaN semantics (a name I made up, meaning that what payload is produced from NaN inputs is unspecified). Further, any-NaN semantics leave room to make non-breaking changes in the future if the landscape shifts such that people do actually care about payloads. It's also robust to hardware that makes unusual NaN propagation choices (I don't think IEEE754 dictates a specific semantic they must follow).

Up to now, almost every function has been implemented with an input-NaN semantic (another name I made up, specifying that the payload of one NaN input is propagated to the output). This is also what's usually (but perhaps not always?) used by hardware-native operations. In fact, our current input-NaN semantic is usually contingent on hardware input-NaN semantics.

There is a risk that this results in re-implementations of functions that "break" existing behaviors if somebody really did rely on payload propagation. For example, I believe there is a faster `min` -- but maybe not `max` -- on x86 if you're willing to mangle payloads. But there was never any formal guarantee and I'm not sure that anybody ever cared.

Is the proposal to document this centrally or on a per-function basis? Per-function seems like it would be a never-finished job and add noise to docstrings, so I'd propose to only document it centrally. Functions which have notable deviations should be documented locally. For example, that `hypot` is not poisoned by NaN in the presence of Inf.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1419414441/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Further,
> [The document linked by a later poster](https://grouper.ieee.org/groups/msc/ANSI_IEEE-Std-754-2019/background/nan-propagation.pdf) suggests that "The current standard specifies that if an operation has multiple NaN inputs, then the result should be one of the input NaNs. The standard does not specify which one." I assume this extends to unary functions as well.

If true, this would mean that to reject payload propagation semantics would be to violate IEEE754 semantics on any function defined therein. I'm not excited about this prospect. 

Let's talk cost/benefit. Are there functions that we would implement differently with loosened NaN semantics? I mentioned a small optimization of `min` on x86 (not aarch64) but it wouldn't be game-changing. Any others?

It seems that, if anything, we might have to document a general policy (although perhaps not a strict guarantee) that a NaN output resulting from one or more NaN inputs should include the payload of one of the NaN inputs. We'd have to adhere to this policy for IEEE754 functions but probably should in other cases as well.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1419567899/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

propagation","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":115858,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTU4NTg=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/performance","name":"performance","color":"d7e102","default":false,"description":"Must go faster"},{"id":171885699,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNzE4ODU2OTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/maths","name":"maths","color":"009800","default":false,"description":"Mathematical functions"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2023-02-01T16:37:49Z","updated_at":"2023-02-07T14:43:55Z","closed_at":"2023-02-07T13:03:28Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"For any `y` except `-0.0` or `NaN`, it holds that `min(x,y) === min(y,x) === ifelse(y<x,y,x)`.
For any `y` except `+0.0` or `NaN`, it holds that `min(x,y) === min(y,x) === ifelse(y<=x,y,x)`.
For any `y` except `-0.0` or `NaN`, it holds that `max(x,y) === max(y,x) === ifelse(y>=x,y,x)`.
For any `y` except `+0.0` or `NaN`, it holds that `max(x,y) === max(y,x) === ifelse(y>x,y,x)`.
~~For `isnan(y)` it holds that `isequal(min(x,y),y)` and `isequal(max(x,y),y)`. Note the lack of guaranteed `===` (except when `x` is not-NaN) so this may not be a desirable substitution.~~ see next line for better
For `x` and/or `y`  is `NaN`, it holds that `min(x,y) === max(x,y) === x-y` (may change with implementation of `min`/`max`).

In other words, when any `min`/`max` operation involves a known value (or a value known *not* be be one of a small set of values), it is possible to use the ternary definitions of these functions (or subtraction if known `NaN`). On x86, the ternary versions require just 1 instruction (`<` or `>`) or 2 instructions (`<=` or `>=`) while the general versions require roughly 8 (see #41709). As of #47814, aarch64 does not suffer this drawback as it has native support for our desired `min`/`max` semantics.

This means that common operations such as `max(x,c)` can be made much faster (on x86) than they currently are when `c` is known (for example, a compile-time constant). However, on aarch64 we might want to avoid this substitution as it might not be better than the natively supported general version.

One can make these substitutions manually, but it'd be pretty slick if the compiler could do this automatically. I don't know what it takes to impart this sort of optimization. This might be LLVM territory? I see this as similar to `sqrt(abs(x))` eliding the check-and-error for negative arguments to `sqrt`, but that similarity may be superficial.

For example, on an x86 compare the following equivalent functions:
```
julia> code_native(x->max(1.0,x),Tuple{Float64};debuginfo=:none)
# shortened for brevity
        movabs  rax, offset .LCPI0_0
        vmovsd  xmm2, qword ptr [rax]           # xmm2 = mem[0],zero
        vsubsd  xmm1, xmm2, xmm0
        vmovq   rax, xmm1
        test    rax, rax
        jns     .LBB0_2
# %bb.1:                                # %top
        vmovapd xmm2, xmm0
.LBB0_2:                                # %top
        vcmpordsd       xmm0, xmm0, xmm0
        vblendvpd       xmm0, xmm1, xmm2, xmm0
        ret

julia> code_native(x->ifelse(1.0>x,1.0,x),Tuple{Float64};debuginfo=:none)
# shortened for brevity
        movabs  rax, offset .LCPI0_0
        vmovsd  xmm1, qword ptr [rax]           # xmm1 = mem[0],zero
        vmaxsd  xmm0, xmm1, xmm0
        ret
```

can be made faster for 3+ arguments.

```julia
using BenchmarkTools

t3 = ntuple(_->randn(), 3)
t4 = ntuple(_->randn(), 4)
t8 = ntuple(_->randn(), 8)
t16 = ntuple(_->randn(), 16)

@btime hypot($t3...)
@btime hypot($t4...)
@btime hypot($t8...)
@btime hypot($t16...)

# master
  9.810 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  10.110 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  19.759 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  50.203 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

# SHA 1a0340c
  7.808 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  8.008 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  10.010 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  14.529 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

The notable savings here come from casting to integers before computing `maximum(abs,...)` (same result but faster comparisons) and re-associating the final sum (see #48129).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48130/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48130/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1421044651","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/48130#issuecomment-1421044651","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48130","id":1421044651,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5Us2ur","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-02-07T16:18:49Z","updated_at":"2023-02-07T16:18:49Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"So I'm guessing the issue here is that `@fastmath` is not defined at the point this code is loaded.  sqrt(x)

+    UT = uinttype(T)

+end

```julia
scale = inv(scale)
# much code
return h*scale*oneunit(axu)
```

But to your question, I've been playing with fast scaling in trying to improve `(/)(::ComplexF64,::ComplexF64)`. I don't have my actual implementation handy, but I was using something very close to this:
```julia
function inv_pow2(x::T) where T<:Base.IEEEFloat
	u = reinterpret(Unsigned, x)
	# floatmin(T) <= x <= inv(floatmin(T)) || error("out of range!") # ensure externally
	# u == u & Base.exponent_mask(T) || error("not a power of 2!") # ensure externally
	xinv = reinterpret(T, 2reinterpret(Unsigned, one(T)) - u)
	return xinv
end

is [defined as](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/b0ac45f3fbc8501c8b3909e838103c88114b29b6/base/regex.jl#L189-L191)
```jl
struct RegexMatch <: AbstractMatch
    match::SubString{String}
    captures::Vector{Union{Nothing,SubString{String}}}
    offset::Int
    offsets::Vector{Int}
    regex::Regex
end
```
which *requires* the `match` to be a `SubString{String}`

I encountered this in JuliaStrings/StringViews.jl#14 — it means that, even though PCRE regex searching is implemented for a `StringView`, the returned `RegexMatch` must *duplicate* the match string into a `String`.  This sucks and will require me to re-implement my own `RegexMatch` with a 1-line change.

Ideally, this would be defined as:
```jl
struct RegexMatch{T} <: AbstractMatch
    match::SubString{T}
    captures::Vector{Union{Nothing,SubString{T}}}
    offset::Int
    offsets::Vector{Int}
    regex::Regex
end
```
instead, to support a wider range of string types. But I guess that would be a breaking change, so it would have to wait for Julia 2.0?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48617/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48617/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1426007765","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48617#issuecomment-1426007765","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48617","id":1426007765,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5U_ybV","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-02-10T15:53:47Z","updated_at":"2023-02-10T15:53:47Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Is this actually breaking? ```julia-repl
julia> quote (a = 1), 2 end # malformed NamedTuple
quote
    #= REPL[418]:1 =#
    (a = 1, 2)
end

julia> quote (a = 1), (b = 2) end # NamedTuple
quote
    #= REPL[420]:1 =#
    (a = 1, b = 2)
end
```
It seems that the parser will blow through parentheses when attempting to parse a `NamedTuple` and will attempt to parse a `NamedTuple` even without surrounding parentheses.

Should `NamedTuple` construction be possible without surrounding parentheses? Should it be allowed to drill through parentheses to find a fieldname or should the fieldname be required to exist at top-level within the `NamedTuple` expression?

functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":14,"created_at":"2023-02-04T16:58:58Z","updated_at":"2023-02-10T23:24:01Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"There are a bunch of different NaN values. `reinterpret(Float64, reinterpret(UInt64, NaN) + 1)` and `NaN` are two examples.

NaNs propagate through floating point operations. `sin(NaN)` must be `NaN` in the sense of `isnan(sin(NaN))`, but which NaN should it return? Must it return the canonical `NaN`? Must it return its input? May it return some other number that `isnan` for performance reasons? These questions come up for most math functions, `min`/`max`/`sort`, and possibly others.

I propose to explicitly document that mathematical functions (e.g. `sin`, `hypot`, `min`) will produce an NaN result on NaN input but that which NaN is produced is an implementation detail.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48523/reactions","total_count":5,"+1":5,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48523/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1426485910","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48523#issuecomment-1426485910","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48523","id":1426485910,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5VBnKW","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-02-10T23:24:01Z","updated_at":"2023-02-10T23:24:01Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> but still try to return the first NaN argument when possible

I disagree. I would say "one of the NaN arguments when possible." Anything more than that is going to be untenable. For example, the following two operations are implemented using a single native x86 instructions yet don't return the same positional operand when given two NaNs:
```julia-repl
julia> x = reinterpret(Float64,-1); y = reinterpret(Float64,-2);

julia> reinterpret(Int, x+y) # vaddsd
-1

julia> reinterpret(Int, ifelse(x<y,x,y)) # vminsd
-2
```
Hardware does not take strong positions on this so supporting any positional preference would be a pain even on a single architecture (to say nothing of multiple). Plus, compilers are free to fiddle with some operations (e.g., `a+b` for `b+a`) so inlining and other factors can change behavior even with the hardware held constant.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1426485910/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Performance is not quite as good as that PR but remains comparable for small numbers of inputs. The performance loss will be recovered if #48129 is resolved. In any case, it's a big improvement over master on an x86-64 architecture.

```julia-repl
using BenchmarkTools

t3 = ntuple(_->randn(), 3)
t4 = ntuple(_->randn(), 4)
t8 = ntuple(_->randn(), 8)
t16 = ntuple(_->randn(), 16)

@btime hypot($t3...)
@btime hypot($t4...)
@btime hypot($t8...)
@btime hypot($t16...)

# master
  9.810 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  10.110 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  19.759 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  50.203 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

# this PR
  8.008 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  7.508 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  12.613 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  21.565 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

The main difference is a replacing `maximum(abs,x)` with a specialized version for `IEEEFloats`. This performance difference is significant on x86-64 but may not exist on aarch64 since it has a native `max` #48130 but without the `@fastmath` that was causing build problems. Performance is not quite as good as that PR but remains comparable for small numbers of inputs. The performance loss will be recovered if #48129 is resolved. In any case, it's a big improvement over master on an x86-64 architecture.

```julia-repl
using BenchmarkTools

t3 = ntuple(_->randn(), 3)
t4 = ntuple(_->randn(), 4)
t8 = ntuple(_->randn(), 8)
t16 = ntuple(_->randn(), 16)

@btime hypot($t3...)
@btime hypot($t4...)
@btime hypot($t8...)
@btime hypot($t16...)

# master
  9.810 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  10.110 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  19.759 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  50.203 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

# this PR
  8.008 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  7.508 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  12.613 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  21.565 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

The main difference is a replacing `maximum(abs,x)` with a specialized version for `IEEEFloats`. Using power-of-two normalization allows for this algorithm to avoid division operations. The speedup is significant for 3 arguments but is small after that. The `code_native` did not offer an explanation - I can only assume that more inputs allowed it to bury the latency of the divisions.

```julia-repl
## see original post for benchmark

# previous timings
  8.008 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  7.508 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  12.613 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  21.565 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

# new timings
  5.500 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  7.608 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  11.411 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  19.960 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

This change includes a new internal function, currently called `Base.Math.scaleinv`. {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1103541455","pull_request_review_id":1294440339,"id":1103541455,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5BxrTP","diff_hunk":"@@ -841,6 +841,15 @@ function _hypot(x::NTuple{N,<:Number}) where {N}
     end
 end
 
+function _hypot(x::NTuple{N,T}) where {N,T<:IEEEFloat}
+    infT = convert(T, Inf)
+    x = abs.(x) # doesn't change result but enables computational shortcuts
+    any(==(infT), x) && return infT # return Inf even if an argument is NaN
+    maxabs = reinterpret(T, maximum(z -> reinterpret(Signed, z), x)) # for abs(::IEEEFloat) values, a ::BitInteger cast does not change the result","path":"base/math.jl","position":9,"original_position":8,"commit_id":"3e795f7c4ae6d7160c1d918eb6f451b71a0127ed","original_commit_id":"188e62cd5482dfc75a744955e29e9d169aae97f3","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I was worried that this would cause a problem in cases where `sqrt(x^2) != abs(x)` (e.g., for `hypot(x,0,0)`). I've since tried every Float32 between 1f0 and 2f0 (WLOG: all values) and billions of Float64s and this never happens, so it appears this won't cause incorrectly rounded calls with a lone nonzero.

(re-do)","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Redoing #48130 but without the `@fastmath` that was causing build problems. Performance is not quite as good as that PR but remains comparable for small numbers of inputs. The performance loss will be recovered if #48129 is resolved. In any case, it's a big improvement over master on an x86-64 architecture.

```julia-repl
using BenchmarkTools

t3 = ntuple(_->randn(), 3)
t4 = ntuple(_->randn(), 4)
t8 = ntuple(_->randn(), 8)
t16 = ntuple(_->randn(), 16)

@btime hypot($t3...)
@btime hypot($t4...)
@btime hypot($t8...)
@btime hypot($t16...)

# master
  9.810 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  10.110 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  19.759 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  50.203 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

# this PR
  5.500 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  7.608 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  11.411 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
  19.960 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

The biggest difference is a replacing `maximum(abs,x)` with a specialized version for `IEEEFloats`. This performance difference is significant on x86-64 but may not exist on aarch64 since it has a native `max` instruction. This also uses fast scaling values (rather than division) to achieve additional {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48665","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48665/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48665/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48665/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48665","id":1582764974,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5eVxOu","number":48665,"title":"Feature request: maybe-break","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2023-02-13T17:26:34Z","updated_at":"2023-02-13T22:48:34Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"`break` is a useful construct. One of its uses is to "short circuit" operations, exiting evaluation early if the outcome becomes known early. For example, `any` and `all` make use of it.

However, there is a cost. A loop with a `break` condition is not SIMD-able and doesn't unroll as well. This is because execution is required to terminate immediately upon reaching `break`. This makes them poorly suited for checking exceptional cases that usually don't break early. A common example of this is a check for `any(isnan, x)` that branches to some alternative code. The performance implications can be considerable. Consider the following semantically-equivalent operations:
```julia-repl
## version 1.8.0

julia> x = fill(1.0, 1000);

julia> @btime all(>=(0), $x)
  454.315 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
true

julia> @btime count(>=(0), $x) == length($x)
  59.003 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
true
```
For simple predicates like this one, in the case that the whole input must be evaluated, the cost of eager `break` is considerable: almost an 8x slowdown. Since `all` uses short-circuiting, it can be expected to exit almost instantly if a `false` is found. Despite this, unless a `false` appears in the first ~1/8 of the input, `all` will be strictly slower than the `count`-based version that exhaustively checks every element.

I propose a "maybe-break" construct that gives execution the *option* to exit the block but does not require it. This would permit execution to only check the exit condition periodically, enabling SIMD and easier unrolling, while still allowing an early exit. For example, this would permit the operation to evaluate a chunk of inputs and *then* decide whether to `break`. If the predicate were expensive, such that it was not useful to SIMD or unroll, it would break immediately.

Maybe-break provides a best-of-both-worlds opportunity: operations that don't terminate quickly can benefit from SIMD/unrolling while operations that do terminate quickly only run a small number of excess evaluations. Operations that are expensive can continue to exit immediately upon a decisive result. It might be necessary to manually opt in to (or out of, but that would be more likely to be breaking) maybe-break in functions like `any`/`all` in case one really does require strict short-circuit semantics.

This feature would require communicating this maybe-break desire to LLVM. I don't know whether LLVM supports such a construct at this point or how difficult it might be to add.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48665/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48665/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1428809599","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48665#issuecomment-1428809599","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48665","id":1428809599,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5VKed_","user":{"login":"mikmoore","id":95002244,"node_id":"U_kgDOBamehA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95002244?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore","html_url":"https://github.com/mikmoore","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mikmoore/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-02-13T22:48:34Z","updated_at":"2023-02-13T22:48:34Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"If the compiler could figure out this transformation automatically in some common cases, that would obviously be even better.
